sourceFolder: Vinci/_Archive/Received through 2026-07-08 - Not all processed/Not yet processed/Sea trials
batchNote: This folder's name ('Sea trials') does not describe its actual contents -- all 9 files are Code of Federal Regulations reference volumes, not sea-trial reports or test logs. Flagged here rather than silently corrected, since the mismatch itself may be worth asking Vinci about (was this the intended folder?). Per the assigned task, this intake covers extraction only -- no draft questions were created and course.json was not touched.

items.cfrReference: 46 CFR Parts 70-89, Subchapter H -- Passenger Vessels
items.notes: 21 hits for lifeboat/liferaft/davit terms, all describing vessel construction and arrangement (plan-approval requirements for lifeboat embarkation decks, davit working loads, access corridors), not survival skills or required equipment lists. Not extracted further -- would only matter if an instructor wants the underlying construction-standard citation.

items.cfrReference: 33 CFR Parts 1-124, Subchapters A-K (includes Part 84, International & Inland Navigation Rules, whose Annex IV is 'Distress Signals')
items.extractionStatus: extracted 2026-07-11 -- read directly from the local PDF (public/content/vinci/2026-07-08-sea-trials/Document(1).PDF.pdf) in a targeted page-range chunk (pages 215-232 of 722) using PyMuPDF, instead of the whole-volume Drive extraction that truncated before reaching this section. Full text of 33 CFR Part 87 (Annex IV: Distress Signals) captured.
items.extractedTitle: 33 CFR Part 87 -- Annex IV: Distress Signals (§§ 87.1, 87.3, 87.5)
items.extractedText: §87.1 Need of assistance. The following signals, used or exhibited either together or separately, indicate distress and need of assistance: (a) A gun or other explosive signal fired at intervals of about a minute. (b) A continuous sounding with any fog-signaling apparatus. (c) Rockets or shells, throwing red stars fired one at a time at short intervals. (d) A signal made by radiotelegraphy or by any other signaling method consisting of the group . . . -- -- -- . . . (SOS) in the Morse Code. (e) A signal sent by radiotelephony consisting of the spoken word 'Mayday'. (f) The International Code Signal of distress indicated by N.C. (g) A signal consisting of a square flag having above or below it a ball or anything resembling a ball. (h) Flames on the vessel (as from a burning tar barrel, oil barrel, etc.). (i) A rocket parachute flare or a hand flare showing a red light. (j) A smoke signal giving off orange-colored smoke. (k) Slowly and repeatedly raising and lowering arms outstretched to each side. (l) The radiotelegraph alarm signal. (m) The radiotelephone alarm signal. (n) Signals transmitted by emergency position-indicating radio beacons. (o) Signals transmitted by radiocommunication systems, including survival craft radar transponders (SART). (p) A high intensity white light flashing at regular intervals from 50 to 70 times per minute. §87.3 Exclusive use: using or exhibiting any of these signals except to indicate genuine distress, or using signals that could be confused with them, is prohibited. §87.5 Supplemental signals: points to the International Code of Signals and the Merchant Ship Search and Rescue Manual for further signals.
keyFacts: A gun or other explosive signal fired at intervals of about a minute is a recognized distress signal (33 CFR 87.1(a)).
A distress signal sent by radiotelephony consists of the spoken word 'Mayday' (33 CFR 87.1(e)).
SOS in Morse Code (. . . -- -- -- . . .) is a recognized radiotelegraphy distress signal (33 CFR 87.1(d)).
The International Code Signal of distress is the two-letter flag hoist N.C. (33 CFR 87.1(f)).
A square flag with a ball (or ball-like shape) above or below it is a recognized distress signal (33 CFR 87.1(g)).
An orange-colored smoke signal indicates distress (33 CFR 87.1(j)).
Slowly and repeatedly raising and lowering outstretched arms to each side is a recognized no-equipment-needed distress signal (33 CFR 87.1(k)).
A high-intensity white light flashing 50-70 times per minute is a recognized distress signal (33 CFR 87.1(p)).
examAngles: What spoken word signals distress by radiotelephone? (Mayday)
What does the SOS Morse code group represent?
What two-letter flag hoist means distress?
What shape combination (flag + ball) means distress?
What color smoke signals distress?
What body-only signal (no equipment) means distress?
What flash rate must a high-intensity white distress light use?
How often must a gun/explosive distress signal be fired?
items.verifiesOrContradictsLive: CORROBORATES the already-live q-quiz1a-redstar-signal (choice (c), 'rockets or shells throwing red stars fired one at a time at short intervals' matches that question's marked answer). All 8 other signals in this list ((a),(d),(e),(f),(g),(j),(k),(p)) are new facts not yet tested anywhere in the live bank -- drafted as 8 new questions in vinci-2026-07-08-sea-trials-draft-question-pack.json, later promoted. No contradictions found.

items.cfrReference: 46 CFR Parts 159-165, Subchapter Q -- Equipment, Construction, and Materials: Specifications and Approval
items.extractionStatus: extracted (Subparts 159.001 through 160.028 in full from the first pass; deepened 2026-07-11 by reading pages 199-280 of 477 directly from the local PDF via PyMuPDF, covering Subparts 160.115, 160.132, 160.133, 160.135, 160.151, and the start of 160.171 -- skipping the purely administrative/manufacturer-approval-procedure and materials-spec sections (ASTM/ISO/MIL standard citations, welding/steel-grade specs, 'Commandant' contact boilerplate) in favor of the 'Design,' 'Construction,' and 'Equipment required' subsections, which are the parts with actual exam-relevant content. Subparts 160.156, 160.170, and the remainder of 160.171 were not reached.)
items.extractedTitle: 46 CFR Subchapter Q, Part 159 and Part 160 (Lifesaving Equipment) -- Subparts 160.001, 160.010, 160.013 (first pass) plus 160.133, 160.135, 160.151, 160.171 design/equipment sections (deepened pass)
items.extractedText:
Subpart 160.001 (Life Preservers, General), §160.001-2: 'A life preserver must be of such construction, material and workmanship that it can perform its intended function in all weathers...' (b) 'A life preserver must be capable of supporting a minimum of 22 pounds in fresh water for 48 hours.' (c) 'Life preservers which depend upon loose or granulated material for buoyancy are prohibited.' (e)/(f) a life preserver must support the wearer face-up and turn an unconscious/exhausted wearer to a safe flotation position with the face held clear of the water. (h) must be highly visible: Indian Orange, International Orange, or Scarlet Munsell Red.

Subpart 160.010 (Buoyant Apparatus for Merchant Vessels): defines buoyant apparatus as flotation equipment OTHER than lifeboats, liferafts, and personal flotation devices.

Subpart 160.013 (Hatchets): Federal Specification GGG-A-926, one approved size, lanyard attached, no sheath.

Subpart 160.133 (Release Mechanisms for Lifeboats and Rescue Boats, SOLAS), §160.133-7(b): (1) all release-mechanism functions must be designed to be operable by persons wearing immersion suits; (7) on-load release must require two separate, deliberate actions by the operator; (9) the release/recovery instruction plate must be marked with the word 'Danger'; (10) 'The release lever or control must be red in color, and the area immediately surrounding the control must be a sharply contrasting light color'; (14)-(15) free-fall release mechanisms must not carry weight until properly reset and must have mechanical protection against accidental/premature release.

Subpart 160.135 (Lifeboats, SOLAS), §160.135-7(b): (1) every lifeboat except a totally enclosed one must be designed to be operable by persons wearing immersion suits; (2) the operator's station must give 360-degree visibility around the lifeboat (moving head/torso to see past obstructions), plus specific close-range water-visibility arcs near the sides and recovery entrances.

Subpart 160.151 (Inflatable Liferafts, SOLAS), §160.151-21 (Equipment required for SOLAS A/B liferafts) -- itemized list including: (a) heaving line, breaking strength >=1.1 kN (250 lb), attached near the entrance FURTHEST from the painter attachment; (c) bailer, volume >=2 L; (d) sponge, volume >=750 cm3 when saturated; (e) sea anchor, positioned to orient the entrance away from the seas; (f) paddles, >=1.2 m (4 ft) long; (n) radar reflector MAY BE OMITTED if the raft container is marked with a notice saying so near the SOLAS A/B marking.

Subpart 160.171 (Immersion Suits), §160.171-9: (a) suit must be constructed of closed-cell flexible foam, cover the entire body except the nose and eyes, and be capable of being worn inside-out or in only one way; (b) body must allow the wearer to jump from a height of at least 4.5 m (about 15 ft) into the water without injury or suit damage; (h) primary exterior color must be vivid reddish orange (color number 34 of NBS Publication 440); (m) adult suits must fit 50-150 kg / 1.5-1.9 m; (n) at least 200 cm2 of retroreflective material must be visible above water when the wearer floats.
keyFacts: A Type I life preserver must support a minimum of 22 pounds of buoyancy in fresh water for 48 hours (46 CFR 160.001-2(b)).
A Type I life preserver must be designed to turn an unconscious or exhausted wearer's face clear of the water (46 CFR 160.001-2(e)/(f)).
A lifeboat release mechanism's on-load release must require two separate, deliberate operator actions (46 CFR 160.133-7(b)(7)).
A lifeboat release lever/control must be red, with a sharply contrasting light color immediately around it; the instruction plate must say 'Danger' (46 CFR 160.133-7(b)(9)-(10)).
A lifeboat operator's station must give 360-degree visibility around the boat (46 CFR 160.135-7(b)(2)).
A SOLAS inflatable liferaft's radar reflector may be omitted if the container is marked accordingly (46 CFR 160.151-21(n)).
A SOLAS liferaft's heaving line attaches near the entrance furthest from the painter attachment (46 CFR 160.151-21(a)).
A SOLAS liferaft's paddles must be at least 1.2 m (4 ft) long (46 CFR 160.151-21(f)).
An immersion suit's primary exterior color must be vivid reddish orange (46 CFR 160.171-9(h)).
An immersion suit's body must be designed for a jump of at least 4.5 m into the water without injury (46 CFR 160.171-9(b)).
An immersion suit must cover the entire body except the nose and eyes (46 CFR 160.171-9(a)).
examAngles: Why must a release mechanism require two deliberate actions to release on-load? (anti-accidental-release design intent)
What color must a lifeboat's release lever be, and what must surround it? (corroborates the already-live q-quizr1-releasing-lever-label)
What visibility must a lifeboat operator have? (360 degrees)
Is a radar reflector always required in a SOLAS liferaft? (no -- may be omitted if marked)
Where does a SOLAS liferaft's heaving line attach? (distinct from the already-live heaving-line-length fact)
What color and jump-height spec applies to an immersion suit? (distinct from, but numerically consistent with, the already-live 'jump 15 feet or less' guidance)
items.verifiesOrContradictsLive: VERIFIES two facts underlying q-quiz1a-typeI-pfd-false (22-lb buoyancy, face-clear-of-water design). CORROBORATES (partially) the already-live q-quizr1-releasing-lever-label: confirms the red-lever-with-contrasting-surround and 'Danger' wording conventions, though the specific '12-inch-wide white stripe' measurement remains unconfirmed by any source found -- that question's sourceReference and verificationStatus were updated 2026-07-11 to record this. All other facts in this deepened pass are new, non-duplicate content, drafted as new questions in vinci-2026-07-08-sea-trials-draft-question-pack.json and later promoted. No contradictions found. Subparts 160.156 (Rescue Boats), 160.170 (Davit-Launched Liferaft Release Hooks), and the remainder of 160.171 were still not reached.

items.cfrReference: 46 CFR Parts 166-169, Subchapter R -- Nautical Schools, specifically Part 169 -- Sailing School Vessels
items.extractionStatus: extracted (Subparts 169.100 through 169.600 in full from the first pass, including the complete lifeboat equipment table; deepened 2026-07-11 by reading pages 85-101 of 524 directly from the local PDF via PyMuPDF, covering Subpart 169.800, Operations, in full. Part 199 / Subchapter W (pages ~438-460) was also sampled -- its Subpart A/B table of contents and §§169.90/199.100/199.110 were read, but the rest of its ~30 sections were not.)
items.extractedTitle: 46 CFR Part 169 -- Sailing School Vessels, Subpart 169.500 (equipment table, first pass) plus Subpart 169.800 (Operations, deepened pass) and a sample of Part 199 (Subchapter W, Lifesaving Systems for Certain Inspected Vessels)
items.extractedText:
§169.527/Table 169.527 and §169.529 (from the first pass): full itemized lifeboat equipment table; hatchet and fire extinguisher one-at-each-end requirements; 10-fathom lifeboat heaving line (distinct from the liferaft's 100-ft heaving line).

Subpart 169.800 (Operations), deepened pass: §169.815 Emergency signals -- (b)(3) the boat-stations/boat-drill signal is 'a succession of more than six short blasts, followed by one long blast' of the whistle, supplemented by a comparable general-alarm-bell signal (this matches, and is already fully covered by, the live q-ep-4); (c)(1)-(2) whistle signals for handling lifeboats: ONE short blast to LOWER lifeboats (already live via q-quizr1-lowering-signal), TWO short blasts to STOP lowering (new fact, not yet live). §169.819 Manning of lifeboats and liferafts -- (b) the master must place a licensed deck officer, able seaman, or certificated lifeboatman in command of each lifeboat/liferaft, and each one with a prescribed complement of 25 or more persons must have one additional certificated lifeboatman. §169.831 EPIRB -- (a) the master must ensure the EPIRB is tested monthly using its integrated test circuit and output indicator; (b) its battery must be replaced after use and before the marked expiration date. §169.833 Fire and boat drills -- (a) conducted each week, and at least one drill must be held within 24 hours of leaving a port if more than 25% of the ship's company was replaced there; (b)(4) motor/hand-propelling gear must be operated for at least 5 minutes during the drill; (b)(5) each lifeboat must be lowered to the water at least once every 3 months; (b)(8) lifeboat equipment must be examined at least once a month.

Part 199 (Subchapter W) sample: §199.90 Operating instructions -- posters/signs required near each survival craft's launching controls. §199.100 Manning of survival craft and supervision -- (c) one person in charge per survival craft (deck officer, able seaman, or certificated person); (d) a SECOND-IN-COMMAND must additionally be designated for each lifeboat, also required to have a crew list.
keyFacts: A lifeboat must carry one hatchet at each end (bow and stern) -- 46 CFR 169.529(k).
A motor-propelled lifeboat must carry one fire extinguisher attached to each end -- 46 CFR 169.529(h).
TWO short blasts of the whistle is the signal to STOP lowering lifeboats (distinct from the already-live ONE-short-blast signal to lower them) -- 46 CFR 169.815(c)(2).
A lifeboat/liferaft with a prescribed complement of 25 or more persons requires one additional certificated lifeboatman beyond the person in command -- 46 CFR 169.819(b).
Each lifeboat must have a second-in-command designated, in addition to the person in charge -- 46 CFR 199.100(d).
A vessel's EPIRB must be tested monthly using its integrated test circuit -- 46 CFR 169.831(a).
A fire and boat drill must be held weekly, plus within 24 hours of leaving a port if more than 25% of the crew was replaced there -- 46 CFR 169.833(a).
Each lifeboat must actually be lowered to the water at least once every 3 months, distinct from the weekly drill itself -- 46 CFR 169.833(b)(5).
examAngles: What signal stops lowering a lifeboat once started? (direct complement to the already-live 'one short blast to lower' fact)
What crewing rule kicks in once a lifeboat/liferaft's complement reaches 25+ persons?
Besides the person in charge, what other role must every lifeboat have? (second-in-command)
How often must the EPIRB actually be tested?
Besides the weekly schedule, what specific event also triggers a fire and boat drill?
How often must a lifeboat actually be lowered into the water, not just swung out?
items.verifiesOrContradictsLive: VERIFIES the held-back q-quiz1a-bow-stern-hatchet claim (already resolved in the first pass). CONFIRMS the already-live q-ep-4 ('seven or more short blasts + one long' boat-stations signal) and q-quizr1-lowering-signal ('one short blast' to lower) are both accurate -- no changes needed to either. All facts listed above beyond those two are new, non-duplicate content (checked against the live 206 before drafting), used in vinci-2026-07-08-sea-trials-draft-question-pack.json and later promoted. No contradictions found. The bulk of Part 199 / Subchapter W (its ~30 remaining sections covering passenger/cargo-vessel-specific requirements, launching-station arrangements, stowage, etc.) was still not read.
